_meta.authoringNotes: Citations verified against APA Practice Guideline for Treatment of Major Depressive Disorder (2010, 2nd edition), Cuijpers P World Psychiatry 2020 (PMID 32931085), STAR*D landmark trial (Rush 2006), and NICE NG222 Depression in Adults guideline (2022).

h1Title: Mood Disorder: Symptoms, Causes, Diagnosis, and Treatment
heroOverview: Mood disorders are a family of psychiatric conditions defined by sustained disturbances in emotional state that impair functioning, including major depressive disorder, persistent depressive disorder (dysthymia), bipolar I and II disorders, cyclothymic disorder, premenstrual dysphoric disorder, and substance- or medical-condition-induced mood disorders. Globally, mood disorders affect roughly 280 million people; major depression alone affects 8.4% of US adults annually (NSDUH 2022) and is the leading cause of disability worldwide. Onset typically occurs between adolescence and the mid-thirties, with women diagnosed twice as often as men in unipolar depression and equal rates in bipolar disorder. Symptoms range from persistent low mood and loss of interest through racing thoughts and elevated mood, with significant overlap that often delays accurate diagnosis. Evidence-based treatment combining psychotherapy and medication achieves remission in 60-80% of patients within 6-12 months when matched to the correct mood disorder subtype.

keyStats.prevalence: Major depression 8.4% of US adults annually (NSDUH 2022); bipolar disorder 2.8% lifetime
keyStats.demographics: Unipolar depression 2x more common in women; bipolar I roughly equal across sexes
keyStats.avgAge: Mean age of onset 25 for major depression, 18-25 for bipolar I, late adolescence for cyclothymia
keyStats.globalCases: Approximately 280 million people globally with depression (WHO 2023)

definition: Mood disorders (ICD-10: F30-F39) comprise a DSM-5 diagnostic category of psychiatric conditions defined by sustained, clinically significant disturbance of mood that impairs functioning. The major subtypes are: major depressive disorder (single or recurrent episode of depressed mood plus neurovegetative symptoms for at least two weeks), persistent depressive disorder formerly dysthymia (chronic low-grade depression lasting at least two years in adults), bipolar I disorder (history of at least one manic episode), bipolar II disorder (at least one major depressive episode plus at least one hypomanic episode without full mania), cyclothymic disorder (chronic fluctuating subthreshold hypomanic and depressive symptoms for at least two years), premenstrual dysphoric disorder (cyclical luteal-phase mood disturbance), and substance- or medical-condition-induced mood disorder. Diagnosis follows DSM-5-TR criteria with full psychiatric history, mental status examination, and exclusion of medical contributors (thyroid disease, anemia, vitamin B12 deficiency, sleep apnea, medication side effects). The disorders share dysregulation of monoamine neurotransmitters, HPA axis hyperactivity, neuroinflammation, and altered limbic-cortical circuitry. Care is led by psychiatrists, primary care physicians, psychologists, social workers, and care coordinators in integrated mental health teams.

typesClassification.description: At least two weeks of depressed mood or loss of interest plus four additional symptoms (sleep, appetite, energy, concentration, psychomotor, guilt, suicidality). Lifetime prevalence 16-20% in adults.

Chronic low-grade depression lasting at least two years in adults (one year in children). Combines features of dysthymia and chronic major depression. More disabling than acute depression due to chronicity.
At least one manic episode lasting 7+ days or requiring hospitalization. Depressive episodes are common but not required for diagnosis. Lifetime prevalence 1% globally.
At least one major depressive episode plus at least one hypomanic episode (4+ days of elevated mood without functional impairment or psychosis). Often misdiagnosed as unipolar depression.
Chronic fluctuating mood with subthreshold hypomanic and depressive symptoms for at least two years. Considered a temperament that often precedes bipolar disorder.
Cyclical luteal-phase mood disturbance, irritability, and physical symptoms severe enough to impair functioning. Distinguished from PMS by severity and clear impact on relationships or work.
Mood disturbance directly caused by alcohol, opioids, stimulants, corticosteroids, interferon, thyroid disease, or stroke. Resolves when the underlying cause is treated or substance is discontinued.

primarySymptoms: Persistent low or sad mood most of the day, nearly every day, that does not lift with positive events or distraction.
Loss of interest or pleasure (anhedonia) in activities that previously brought enjoyment — hobbies, social contact, intimacy, work.
Significant change in appetite or weight (5% or more in a month) without intentional dieting.
Sleep disturbance including early morning awakening, frequent awakenings, or excessive sleep that does not feel restorative.
Fatigue or loss of energy nearly every day even after adequate rest.
Feelings of worthlessness or excessive, inappropriate guilt — guilt about past events that exceeds proportion to the actual circumstance.
Difficulty concentrating, indecisiveness, or perceived slowing of thought that interferes with work or daily tasks.
Psychomotor agitation (restlessness, hand-wringing) or retardation (slowed speech and movement) noticeable to others.
Recurrent thoughts of death, suicidal ideation, or suicide attempts — always warrants clinical assessment.

In bipolar disorder, periods of elevated, expansive, or irritable mood with decreased need for sleep, increased goal-directed activity, racing thoughts, pressured speech, distractibility, and risky behavior.
earlyWarningSigns: Persistent low mood lasting more than two weeks that interferes with work, school, or relationships
Loss of interest in previously enjoyed activities, with social withdrawal and reduced motivation
Sleep changes (insomnia or hypersomnia), appetite changes, or unexplained fatigue persisting beyond expected stress reactions
Periods of unusually elevated mood, reduced sleep need, racing thoughts, or rapid spending — early signs of hypomania or mania
Increased alcohol or substance use as self-medication for mood symptoms
emergencySigns: Active suicidal ideation with a plan, means, or recent attempt — immediate emergency department evaluation or crisis line contact
Acute manic episode with psychotic features, agitation, or severely disorganized behavior — requires emergency psychiatric assessment
Severe depression with catatonia, mutism, or refusal to eat or drink — life-threatening, needs hospitalization
Postpartum depression with thoughts of harming the infant — urgent perinatal psychiatric assessment
Serotonin syndrome (tremor, hyperthermia, agitation, clonus) on antidepressants — discontinue and seek emergency care

causes.description: Heritability is roughly 40% for major depression and 70-90% for bipolar disorder. No single gene accounts for risk; hundreds of common variants contribute small effects. A first-degree relative with bipolar disorder raises personal risk roughly 10-fold.

Mood disorders involve dysregulation of monoamine neurotransmitters (serotonin, norepinephrine, dopamine), hyperactivity of the hypothalamic-pituitary-adrenal axis, chronic low-grade inflammation, and altered connectivity in limbic-cortical circuits (amygdala, hippocampus, prefrontal cortex).

causes.cause: Stressful life events and chronic adversity
causes.description: Loss of a loved one, relationship breakdown, job loss, financial strain, and chronic interpersonal conflict precipitate depressive episodes. Childhood adversity (abuse, neglect, household instability) raises adult depression risk roughly 3-fold and worsens treatment response.

Hypothyroidism, vitamin B12 deficiency, stroke, Parkinson disease, cancer, chronic pain, sleep apnea, corticosteroids, interferon, beta-blockers, and varenicline all cause secondary mood symptoms. Workup excludes these before primary mood disorder is confirmed.

Alcohol, opioids, cannabis (especially in young brains), stimulants, and benzodiazepine withdrawal all produce depressive or manic syndromes. Substance-induced mood disorder resolves when the substance is discontinued long enough for clinical equilibrium.

Perinatal depression affects 10-20% of women within a year of childbirth; perimenopausal hormonal changes raise depression risk; premenstrual dysphoric disorder reflects cyclical luteal-phase sensitivity. Hormonal contributions are real but partial; psychosocial factors also play a role.

riskFactors.description: First-degree relative with major depression raises personal risk 2-3 fold; first-degree relative with bipolar raises risk roughly 10-fold. Heritability 40% for depression, 70-90% for bipolar.
Women have 2x higher lifetime depression risk than men; hormonal, reproductive, and psychosocial factors contribute. Bipolar disorder is roughly equally distributed across sexes.

Childhood physical, sexual, or emotional abuse, neglect, parental loss, or household dysfunction raises adult depression risk 3-fold and worsens treatment response. CDC ACE data show dose-response relationship between number of adverse events and adult mental health.
Depression affects 25% of patients with chronic medical illness — diabetes, heart disease, cancer, chronic pain. Bidirectional relationship: depression worsens medical outcomes and vice versa.
Alcohol use disorder doubles depression risk; opioid use disorder triples it. Substance-induced mood disorders are common and require clinical equilibrium for accurate primary diagnosis.

10-20% of women experience perinatal depression; postpartum psychosis in 1-2 per 1000 deliveries is a psychiatric emergency. History of postpartum depression raises recurrence risk 50% with subsequent pregnancies.
Insomnia raises depression risk 2-3 fold and triggers manic episodes in bipolar disorder. Sleep disturbance is both a symptom and a precipitant; treating it lowers relapse risk.
Chronic loneliness raises depression risk roughly 30% and worsens mortality. Social prescribing and peer support interventions show modest benefit.

affectedDemographics: US: 8.4% of adults experienced major depressive episode in past year (NSDUH 2022); 21 million Americans annually
Globally: 280 million people with depression and 40 million with bipolar disorder (WHO 2023)
Onset peak: 25 years for major depression; 18-25 for bipolar I
Adolescents and young adults show rising depression prevalence; 18.6% of US adolescents aged 12-17 had a major depressive episode in 2022 (NSDUH)
diagnosisOverview: Diagnosis follows DSM-5-TR criteria after comprehensive psychiatric assessment including detailed history of mood symptoms, family history, medical history, substance use, suicidality, and current functioning. The clinician distinguishes the specific mood disorder subtype because treatment differs substantially: bipolar disorder requires mood stabilizers rather than antidepressant monotherapy (which can precipitate manic switch). Validated rating scales support diagnosis and severity assessment: PHQ-9 for depression severity (≥10 suggests moderate depression, ≥20 severe), GAD-7 for comorbid anxiety, MDQ or Bipolar Spectrum Diagnostic Scale for bipolar screening, and Columbia Suicide Severity Rating Scale (C-SSRS) for suicide risk. Mental status examination notes appearance, behavior, speech, mood, affect, thought form and content, perceptual abnormalities, cognition, insight, and judgment. Medical workup excludes secondary causes: TSH and free T4, complete blood count, comprehensive metabolic panel, vitamin B12 and folate, vitamin D, urine toxicology in suspected substance involvement, and screening for sleep apnea when fatigue or hypersomnia predominates. Brain imaging is reserved for atypical presentations, first-onset psychosis, or focal neurological signs. Time course is critical: at least two weeks of symptoms is required for major depressive disorder, two years for persistent depressive disorder, seven days for mania, and four days for hypomania. Episodes are described as mild, moderate, severe, or with psychotic features, and bipolar mood disorders are tracked across the lifespan with mood charts. Differential diagnosis includes adjustment disorder, grief, anxiety disorders, ADHD, post-traumatic stress disorder, personality disorders, and secondary medical causes.

diagnosticTests.test: Comprehensive psychiatric interview and mental status examination
diagnosticTests.purpose: Establishes the diagnosis through DSM-5-TR criteria, identifies psychiatric and medical comorbidity, assesses suicide risk, and informs treatment planning.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A 60-90 minute structured conversation with a psychiatrist, primary care clinician, or psychologist. The clinician asks about mood, sleep, appetite, energy, concentration, suicidality, substance use, family history, and current functioning.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Self-administered nine-item screen for depression severity. Score ≥10 suggests moderate depression; ≥20 indicates severe depression. Used for screening, diagnosis, and tracking treatment response.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A brief self-report questionnaire completed in 2-3 minutes. Item 9 specifically asks about suicidal ideation and warrants follow-up if endorsed.

diagnosticTests.test: Mood Disorder Questionnaire (MDQ) and Bipolar Spectrum Diagnostic Scale
diagnosticTests.purpose: Screen for unrecognized bipolar disorder in patients presenting with depression. A positive MDQ (≥7 manic/hypomanic items endorsed with grouping and impairment) prompts further psychiatric assessment.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Self-report questionnaire completed in 5-10 minutes. Positive screens do not confirm bipolar diagnosis but trigger a more detailed clinical interview.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Structured assessment of suicidal ideation, plan, intent, and behavior. Used at every mental health visit to track risk over time.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A 5-10 minute clinician-administered interview. Direct questions about thoughts of death, plans, and prior attempts. Truthful answers support effective care.

diagnosticTests.test: Medical workup: TSH, CBC, CMP, vitamin B12, folate, vitamin D, urine toxicology
diagnosticTests.purpose: Excludes medical contributors to mood symptoms: hypothyroidism, anemia, electrolyte disturbance, vitamin deficiency, substance use.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Standard blood and urine tests; results typically available in 1-3 days. Performed at initial diagnosis and repeated if treatment response is poor.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Identifies obstructive sleep apnea, which can mimic or worsen depressive symptoms. Considered when fatigue or hypersomnia dominate the presentation.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Overnight sleep study in a laboratory or home setting with multiple sensors. Reported within 1-2 weeks.

treatmentOverview: Treatment is tailored to the specific mood disorder subtype, severity, and patient preference. For major depressive disorder, the APA Practice Guideline (2010, 2nd edition) and NICE NG222 (2022) endorse psychotherapy alone for mild depression, combined psychotherapy and antidepressant medication for moderate to severe depression, and antidepressant medication with consideration of inpatient care or electroconvulsive therapy for severe depression with psychotic or suicidal features. First-line antidepressants are selective serotonin reuptake inhibitors (sertraline 50-200 mg, escitalopram 10-20 mg, fluoxetine 20-60 mg, paroxetine 20-60 mg) or serotonin-norepinephrine reuptake inhibitors (venlafaxine XR 75-225 mg, duloxetine 30-120 mg). Response is assessed at 4-6 weeks; the STAR*D trial showed cumulative remission of 67% after up to four sequential treatment steps. For treatment-resistant depression, augmentation with atypical antipsychotics (aripiprazole, brexpiprazole, quetiapine XR), lithium, or thyroid hormone, or switching to bupropion or mirtazapine, is supported by evidence. Esketamine nasal spray and intravenous ketamine offer rapid relief in treatment-resistant cases. Electroconvulsive therapy remains the most effective treatment for severe depression with response rates of 70-90% and is appropriate for catatonia, severe suicidality, refractory disease, and pregnancy-related severe depression. Transcranial magnetic stimulation is an alternative for moderate treatment-resistant disease. For bipolar disorder, mood stabilizers (lithium, valproate, lamotrigine) and second-generation antipsychotics (quetiapine, olanzapine, lurasidone, cariprazine) are first-line; antidepressant monotherapy is avoided because of manic switch risk. Psychotherapy options include cognitive behavioral therapy (Cuijpers meta-analyses confirm efficacy comparable to medication for mild-moderate depression), interpersonal therapy, behavioral activation, and mindfulness-based cognitive therapy for relapse prevention. Long-term continuation treatment for at least 9-12 months after first episode reduces recurrence; lifelong treatment is recommended after three or more episodes or any severe episode. Comprehensive care includes addressing comorbid anxiety, substance use, and medical conditions, sleep hygiene, exercise, and social support.

medicalTreatments.name: Selective serotonin reuptake inhibitors (sertraline 50-200 mg, escitalopram 10-20 mg, fluoxetine 20-60 mg, paroxetine 20-60 mg)
medicalTreatments.description: First-line antidepressants for major depression and persistent depressive disorder. Block serotonin reuptake at the synapse. Generally well tolerated; common side effects include gastrointestinal upset, sexual dysfunction, and discontinuation symptoms.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Remission rates 30-40% at 8 weeks for first SSRI; cumulative remission 67% after four sequential steps in STAR*D trial.

medicalTreatments.name: Serotonin-norepinephrine reuptake inhibitors (venlafaxine XR 75-225 mg, duloxetine 30-120 mg)
medicalTreatments.description: Second-line antidepressants for major depression, particularly with co-occurring chronic pain or significant fatigue. Block both serotonin and norepinephrine reuptake.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Remission rates comparable to or slightly higher than SSRIs; duloxetine has additional FDA indication for diabetic peripheral neuropathic pain.

medicalTreatments.name: Bupropion (immediate or sustained release 150-450 mg daily)
medicalTreatments.description: Norepinephrine-dopamine reuptake inhibitor with low sexual side effect profile. Useful in atypical depression, smoking cessation, and as augmentation when sexual dysfunction limits SSRI use.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Response rates 50-60% in major depression; weight neutral and activating compared with SSRIs.

medicalTreatments.name: Mood stabilizers (lithium 600-1200 mg titrated to level 0.6-1.0 mmol/L, valproate 750-2000 mg, lamotrigine 25-200 mg titrated slowly)
medicalTreatments.description: First-line treatment for bipolar disorder. Lithium reduces suicide risk uniquely; valproate is effective for mixed and rapid-cycling presentations; lamotrigine prevents depressive relapse. Each requires monitoring (lithium levels, valproate liver and platelets, lamotrigine rash surveillance).
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Lithium reduces mood episodes by roughly 50% over 2 years and reduces suicide risk by 60%; valproate similar acute manic efficacy; lamotrigine effective for bipolar depression maintenance.

medicalTreatments.name: Second-generation antipsychotics (quetiapine XR 150-600 mg, olanzapine 5-20 mg, aripiprazole 2-30 mg, lurasidone 20-120 mg, cariprazine 1.5-6 mg)
medicalTreatments.description: Used for bipolar disorder (mania, mixed, maintenance) and as augmentation in treatment-resistant unipolar depression. Quetiapine and lurasidone have specific bipolar depression indications.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Quetiapine reduces manic and depressive symptoms in bipolar disorder by 40-50% versus placebo; aripiprazole augmentation produces remission in 25-30% of SSRI-refractory unipolar depression.

medicalTreatments.name: Esketamine nasal spray (Spravato) 56-84 mg under clinic supervision
medicalTreatments.description: FDA-approved for treatment-resistant depression. Rapid onset within hours, distinct from monoamine antidepressants. Administered in certified clinics with 2-hour post-dose observation due to dissociation and sedation.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Response rates 50-70% within 24-72 hours in treatment-resistant depression; maintenance therapy required to sustain effect.

medicalTreatments.name: Lithium augmentation (300-900 mg daily, titrated to level 0.6-0.8 mmol/L)
medicalTreatments.description: Long-standing evidence-based augmentation for treatment-resistant unipolar depression and a key bipolar maintenance agent. Reduces suicide risk uniquely among mood medications.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Response rates 30-50% as augmentation in treatment-resistant depression; 60% reduction in suicide risk during maintenance treatment of bipolar disorder.

surgicalOptions.name: Electroconvulsive therapy (ECT)
surgicalOptions.description: Brief electrically induced seizure under general anesthesia, delivered 2-3 times weekly for 6-12 sessions. The most effective treatment for severe depression, particularly with psychotic features, catatonia, severe suicidality, or pregnancy. Modern ECT has minimal cognitive side effects with right unilateral electrode placement.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Response rates 70-90% in severe depression; remission rates 50-80%. Effective in catatonia, postpartum depression, and bipolar depression.

surgicalOptions.name: Repetitive transcranial magnetic stimulation (rTMS)
surgicalOptions.description: Non-invasive stimulation of the dorsolateral prefrontal cortex using magnetic pulses. Delivered 5 days weekly for 4-6 weeks. FDA-approved for treatment-resistant unipolar depression; growing evidence in bipolar depression and OCD.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Response rates 30-50% in treatment-resistant depression; remission rates 20-35%. Non-invasive and outpatient.

surgicalOptions.name: Vagus nerve stimulation
surgicalOptions.description: Implanted device delivering intermittent electrical stimulation to the left vagus nerve. Reserved for severe treatment-resistant depression after multiple failed trials. FDA-approved for chronic refractory disease.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Response rates 20-40% after 1 year; slow onset but sustained effect.

surgicalOptions.name: Deep brain stimulation (investigational)
surgicalOptions.description: Surgical implantation of stimulating electrodes in the subgenual cingulate cortex or other targets for refractory severe depression. Research stage; not FDA-approved outside trial protocols.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Response rates 30-50% in case series; randomized trials have shown variable results.

alternativeTreatments.name: Cognitive behavioral therapy (CBT)
alternativeTreatments.description: Structured 12-20 session psychotherapy with strong evidence for efficacy comparable to antidepressant medication in mild-moderate depression and as augmentation in moderate-severe depression (Cuijpers World Psychiatry 2020). Long-term relapse prevention exceeds medication when therapy is completed.

alternativeTreatments.name: Interpersonal therapy (IPT)
alternativeTreatments.description: Focused 12-16 session therapy targeting role transitions, grief, role disputes, and interpersonal deficits. Evidence comparable to CBT in major depression and well validated in perinatal depression.

alternativeTreatments.name: Behavioral activation
alternativeTreatments.description: Scheduling and increasing pleasurable and mastery activities. Effective as monotherapy in mild-moderate depression and as initial component of integrated CBT.

alternativeTreatments.name: Mindfulness-based cognitive therapy (MBCT)
alternativeTreatments.description: Eight-week structured program combining mindfulness meditation with cognitive therapy. Specifically reduces relapse rates by roughly 30% in patients with three or more prior depressive episodes.

whySeeSpecialist: A psychiatrist should be involved when symptoms fail to remit on first-line antidepressant after 6-8 weeks at adequate dose, when bipolar disorder is suspected (any history of manic or hypomanic symptoms), when suicide risk is moderate to high, when psychosis is present, in pregnancy or postpartum mood disorders, when comorbid substance use complicates care, and in any first-onset severe depression. Primary care manages most uncomplicated mild-moderate depression effectively with appropriate stepped care and supervision.
doctorSelectionGuide: Look for a board-certified psychiatrist or psychiatric nurse practitioner with experience in your specific mood disorder subtype (especially bipolar disorder, perinatal psychiatry, or treatment-resistant depression). Access to integrated psychotherapy (CBT, IPT) through the same practice or an established referral network is important. Cultural and linguistic match matters in long-term mental health care.
preventionStrategies: Maintain regular sleep, exercise, and social rhythms — protect against both depressive and manic episodes
Engage in cognitive behavioral therapy or mindfulness-based cognitive therapy after one depressive episode to reduce recurrence
Limit alcohol and avoid substances that destabilize mood (cannabis in young brains, stimulants in bipolar disorder)
Continue maintenance medication for 9-12 months after a first major depressive episode and indefinitely after three or more episodes
Address treatable medical contributors (hypothyroidism, sleep apnea, vitamin deficiencies) promptly
Build and maintain social support networks; engage in regular peer or family contact
Recognize personal warning signs and prodromal symptoms; act early with established crisis plan
lifestyleModifications: Maintain consistent sleep-wake schedule even on weekends — irregular sleep is a major precipitant of mood episodes
Engage in 150 minutes weekly of moderate aerobic activity — exercise has effect sizes comparable to mild antidepressants for mild-moderate depression
Reduce alcohol to within national guidelines and abstain entirely during acute episodes
Build a written relapse-prevention plan with early warning signs, supports, and emergency contacts
Track mood with a daily mood chart or smartphone app to detect early changes
Practice stress-reduction strategies (mindfulness, meditation, breathing exercises) integrated into routine
Engage with peer support groups, online or in-person, for shared experience and accountability

recommended: Mediterranean dietary pattern — meta-analyses show moderate protection against incident depression
Oily fish or omega-3 supplementation (1-2 g daily EPA+DHA) — modest evidence as adjunct in depression
Adequate vitamin D, folate, and B12 — deficiencies worsen mood and treatment response
Regular meals with stable blood glucose to support energy and concentration
Limit caffeine after noon if insomnia is part of the symptom picture
avoid: Excess alcohol — worsens depression, interferes with antidepressants, and triggers manic episodes
Recreational stimulants and cannabis, especially in bipolar disorder
Highly restrictive diets — calorie deficits worsen mood and energy
Very high intake of ultra-processed foods — observational data link to depression risk
Tyramine-rich foods if on MAOI antidepressants (aged cheese, cured meats)

exerciseGuidelines: Aim for 150-300 minutes weekly of moderate aerobic activity, ideally including outdoor exposure to daylight. Resistance training 2-3 times weekly adds antidepressant benefit. Exercise improves mood, sleep, energy, and cognitive function. Even 10-15 minutes of walking helps when starting from inactivity. Engage in social or group exercise where possible to combine activity with connection.
dailyManagement: Take medications at the same time daily — adherence underpins remission and relapse prevention
Track mood, sleep, and triggers with a simple chart or app to detect early changes
Maintain regular contact with mental health provider — therapy or medication review appointments
Engage in at least one pleasurable or mastery activity daily, even when motivation is low (behavioral activation)
Sleep 7-9 hours consistently with stable bedtime and wake time
Limit alcohol and avoid recreational substances
Have a written crisis plan with helpline numbers and emergency contacts available
prognosis: Prognosis varies by mood disorder subtype and adherence to treatment. Major depressive disorder remits with treatment in 60-80% of patients within 6-12 months when adequate therapy is matched to severity. Recurrence is common — 50% after one episode, 70% after two, 90% after three — so maintenance therapy after three or more episodes is recommended indefinitely. Bipolar disorder is a lifelong illness with episodic course; maintenance lithium reduces relapse by 50% and suicide risk by 60%. Persistent depressive disorder responds more slowly but improves with sustained combined treatment. Cyclothymic disorder often persists across the lifespan but rarely causes severe functional impairment. Untreated severe depression carries 15% lifetime suicide risk; bipolar disorder carries roughly 6-7% lifetime suicide risk, both substantially reduced by adequate treatment. Adverse prognostic factors include severe initial episode, psychotic features, comorbid substance use, chronic medical illness, early onset, and poor social support. Favorable factors include early treatment, adherence, social support, regular psychotherapy, and absence of substance use.
recoveryTimeline: Antidepressant response typically emerges over 2-4 weeks with full effect at 6-8 weeks. Psychotherapy benefits are usually evident at 4-8 weeks and continue developing over 12-20 sessions. Bipolar mood stabilizers take 2-4 weeks for full effect; lithium maintenance reduces relapse over the first 6-12 months. ECT shows response in 6-12 sessions over 3-4 weeks. Full functional recovery (returning to work, relationships, normal activities) typically occurs 2-6 months after symptomatic remission.
complications: Suicidal ideation, attempt, or completed suicide — lifetime risk 15% in severe untreated unipolar depression and 6-7% in bipolar disorder
Substance use disorder developing as self-medication for mood symptoms
Disability and unemployment — depression is the leading cause of disability globally
Cardiovascular disease — depression doubles cardiovascular mortality independent of conventional risk factors
Family and relationship breakdown from chronic symptoms
Treatment-resistant depression in 20-30% of patients after multiple medication trials
Manic or hypomanic switch in bipolar disorder treated with antidepressant monotherapy

supportResources.name: National Alliance on Mental Illness (NAMI)
supportResources.description: US patient and family organization with helplines, peer support, and education on all mood and other psychiatric disorders.

supportResources.name: Depression and Bipolar Support Alliance (DBSA)
supportResources.description: US peer-led organization specifically for depression and bipolar disorder. Online and in-person support groups, wellness tools, and education.

supportResources.name: 988 Suicide and Crisis Lifeline (US)
supportResources.description: 24/7 free and confidential support for people in distress. Call or text 988 in the United States.

confusedWithConditions.name: Major Depressive Disorder
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Major depressive disorder is a specific mood disorder subtype with at least one episode of at least two weeks of depressed mood plus four additional symptoms. Mood disorder is the umbrella category that also includes persistent depressive disorder, bipolar disorders, cyclothymia, and substance- or medical-condition-induced mood disorders.

confusedWithConditions.name: Bipolar Disorder
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Bipolar disorder is a specific mood disorder subtype defined by at least one manic or hypomanic episode. It is distinguished from unipolar depression by the history of elevated mood phases; antidepressant monotherapy is avoided because it can trigger manic switch. Mood stabilizers and second-generation antipsychotics are first-line.

confusedWithConditions.name: Generalized Anxiety Disorder
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Generalized anxiety disorder is characterized by persistent excessive worry and physical anxiety symptoms rather than sustained mood disturbance. Anxiety frequently coexists with mood disorders (50-60% comorbidity) but is a distinct diagnostic category with its own treatment approach.

confusedWithConditions.name: Schizophrenia
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Schizophrenia is a primary psychotic disorder defined by hallucinations, delusions, disorganized thought, and negative symptoms persisting for at least 6 months. Mood disorders can include psychotic features during episodes but mood disturbance is primary, with psychosis resolving as mood normalizes.

relatedConditions.name: Major Depressive Disorder
relatedConditions.relevance: The most common mood disorder subtype; major depressive disorder accounts for the largest share of mood disorder diagnoses.

relatedConditions.name: Bipolar Disorder
relatedConditions.relevance: Mood disorder subtype defined by manic or hypomanic episodes; requires distinct treatment with mood stabilizers.

relatedConditions.name: Anxiety
relatedConditions.relevance: Coexists with mood disorders in 50-60% of patients; integrated treatment of both improves outcomes.

relatedConditions.name: Alcohol Use Disorder
relatedConditions.relevance: Doubles depression risk and can produce substance-induced mood disorder; treating substance use often improves mood symptoms.

relatedConditions.name: Opioid Use Disorder
relatedConditions.relevance: Triples depression risk and complicates treatment; integrated addiction and mood care is essential.

faqs.question: What is a mood disorder?
faqs.answer: A mood disorder is a psychiatric condition defined by sustained disturbance of emotional state that impairs functioning.

faqs.question: What are the main types of mood disorders?
faqs.answer: The main types are major depressive disorder (sustained depression), persistent depressive disorder (chronic low-grade depression lasting two or more years), bipolar I disorder (with full manic episodes), bipolar II disorder (with hypomanic and depressive episodes), cyclothymic disorder, premenstrual dysphoric disorder, and substance- or medical-condition-induced mood disorders.

faqs.question: How are mood disorders diagnosed?
faqs.answer: Diagnosis follows DSM-5-TR criteria after comprehensive psychiatric history, mental status examination, and validated scales like the PHQ-9. Medical workup excludes secondary causes including thyroid disease, vitamin deficiencies, and substance effects.

faqs.question: What causes mood disorders?
faqs.answer: Mood disorders arise from genetic vulnerability (heritability 40-90%), dysregulation of monoamine neurotransmitters and the HPA axis, chronic stress and adverse childhood experiences, medical conditions, medications, and substance use.

faqs.question: How are mood disorders treated?
faqs.answer: Treatment combines psychotherapy and medication tailored to the subtype. Major depression responds to SSRIs, SNRIs, bupropion, and cognitive behavioral therapy.

faqs.question: What is the difference between depression and a mood disorder?
faqs.answer: Major depressive disorder is one specific type of mood disorder. The mood disorder category also includes persistent depressive disorder, bipolar disorders, cyclothymic disorder, and substance- or medical-condition-induced mood disorders.

faqs.question: Can mood disorders be cured?
faqs.answer: Mood disorders are not cured in the traditional sense but can enter sustained remission with treatment. Major depression remits in 60-80% of treated patients within 6-12 months. Bipolar disorder is lifelong but well-controlled with maintenance therapy.

faqs.question: How long do antidepressants take to work?
faqs.answer: Antidepressants typically begin to work over 2-4 weeks with full response at 6-8 weeks. Some side effects (gastrointestinal upset, headache) emerge early and resolve within 1-2 weeks. If no benefit is seen at adequate dose by 6-8 weeks, the medication is switched or augmented.

faqs.question: Are mood disorders genetic?
faqs.answer: There is a strong genetic component. Heritability is roughly 40% for major depression and 70-90% for bipolar disorder. A first-degree relative with bipolar disorder raises personal risk roughly 10-fold; with depression, 2-3 fold.

faqs.question: What is the best therapy for mood disorders?
faqs.answer: Cognitive behavioral therapy and interpersonal therapy have the strongest evidence for major depression. Behavioral activation works well in mild-moderate depression. Mindfulness-based cognitive therapy specifically reduces relapse in recurrent depression.

faqs.question: Can children have mood disorders?
faqs.answer: Yes. Major depression and bipolar disorder occur in children and adolescents; 18.6% of US adolescents aged 12-17 had a major depressive episode in 2022 (NSDUH). Diagnosis is more complex in young people due to developmental factors and overlap with ADHD and anxiety.

faqs.question: Is lithium still used for bipolar disorder?
faqs.answer: Yes. Lithium remains the gold-standard mood stabilizer for bipolar disorder. It reduces mood episodes by approximately 50% over 2 years and is the only mood medication shown to reduce suicide risk (by roughly 60%).

faqs.question: What is electroconvulsive therapy?
faqs.answer: Electroconvulsive therapy delivers a brief electrically induced seizure under general anesthesia. It is the most effective treatment for severe depression, with response rates of 70-90%.

faqs.question: Can stress cause a mood disorder?
faqs.answer: Stress is a major precipitant but not the sole cause of mood disorders. Acute stressors can trigger episodes in genetically vulnerable individuals. Chronic stress and adverse childhood experiences raise lifetime risk roughly 3-fold.

faqs.question: How do I know if I have bipolar disorder or just depression?
faqs.answer: Bipolar disorder requires at least one episode of elevated, expansive, or irritable mood with decreased need for sleep, racing thoughts, pressured speech, and risky behavior lasting at least 4 days (hypomania) or 7 days (mania).

faqs.question: What is treatment-resistant depression?
faqs.answer: Treatment-resistant depression is failure to respond to at least two adequate antidepressant trials of different classes.

faqs.question: Should I exercise if I have a mood disorder?
faqs.answer: Yes. Regular exercise has effect sizes comparable to mild antidepressants for mild-moderate depression. Aim for 150-300 minutes weekly of moderate aerobic activity, ideally outdoors with daylight exposure. Resistance training 2-3 times weekly adds benefit. Exercise also improves sleep, energy, and cognitive function and reduces relapse risk.

faqs.question: Can mood disorders affect physical health?
faqs.answer: Yes. Untreated mood disorders raise cardiovascular mortality, worsen diabetes and chronic pain outcomes, and reduce life expectancy by 10-20 years in severe cases. Bidirectional relationships exist with most chronic illness — mood disorders worsen medical outcomes and chronic illness worsens mood. Integrated care addressing both is essential.

faqs.question: When should I see a psychiatrist?
faqs.answer: See a psychiatrist when symptoms fail to remit on first-line antidepressant after 6-8 weeks at adequate dose, when bipolar disorder is suspected, when suicide risk is moderate to high, when psychosis is present, in pregnancy or postpartum mood disorders, when comorbid substance use complicates care, and in any first-onset severe mood disorder.

faqs.question: How much does mood disorder treatment cost?
faqs.answer: Costs vary widely. Generic SSRIs and mood stabilizers are inexpensive (USD 5-20 per month in many markets). Psychotherapy ranges from USD 75-300 per session in the US, often partially covered by insurance. Esketamine nasal spray and ECT are expensive but typically covered for severe disease. In India and other emerging markets, generic medications and public mental health services lower cost substantially.
